diagnostic_signals.explanation: CGWB defines groundwater stress classes based on extraction as a percentage of net annual recharge. In coastal alluvial aquifers, SOGE values above 70% signal Semi-critical or worse conditions. Unlike hard rock aquifers, alluvial coastal aquifers have higher storativity but are vulnerable to seawater intrusion once extraction exceeds recharge, making the SOGE threshold a reliable block-level indicator. Rapid groundwater-level declines (>0.5 m/year) are documented as widespread in dry regions with extensive croplands globally, and coastal alluvial plains under intensive irrigation are particularly susceptible.

condition.qualitative_description: Well depth has increased by more than 2 metres over the available multi-year record, indicating declining static water level in the coastal alluvial zone.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: Deepening well depth is a direct proxy for declining groundwater table. In coastal alluvial plains, a 2m decline over the monitoring period is significant because shallow water tables in these settings are critical for both irrigation and preventing saline intrusion from the sea. Accelerating declines in 30% of the world's regional aquifers have been documented, with coastal alluvial systems particularly at risk due to the dual pressure of extraction and reduced recharge from land-use change.

condition.qualitative_description: Annual delta_g is negative in more than 3 of the last 6 years, and cumulative_g_mm shows a sustained downward trend, indicating chronic excess extraction over recharge in the coastal alluvial system.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: A consistently negative delta_g across multiple years means outflows (ET + runoff) persistently exceed precipitation, leaving no surplus for groundwater recharge. In coastal alluvial plains with sub-humid rainfall (900–1400mm), this is particularly concerning because the monsoon recharge window is relatively short and extraction for rabi and zaid irrigation draws down the aquifer between monsoon seasons. A sustained negative cumulative_g trend confirms that the aquifer is not recovering between seasons.

condition.qualitative_description: Fewer than 5 cumulative SWC works in the micro-watershed over the MGNREGA record, indicating low investment in recharge infrastructure despite the availability of coastal drainage lines and tank systems.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: India has more than 500,000 tanks and ponds historically used to detain surface runoff for irrigation and aquifer recharge, many thousands of years old. Low MGNREGA SWC investment in coastal alluvial plains means that this traditional recharge infrastructure has not been maintained or expanded, amplifying depletion caused by extraction. The Indian government and NGO investment in percolation tanks to infiltrate detained monsoon runoff has been documented as a critical managed aquifer recharge strategy in southern India, and its absence signals a missed recharge opportunity.

overall_reasoning_note: Groundwater stress in coastal alluvial aquifers should be confirmed by at least two of the three primary signals: elevated SOGE classification, deepening well trend, or chronic negative delta_g / declining groundwater recharge trend. A single signal may reflect data noise or seasonal variation. The absence of SWC investment (sig_04) amplifies the other signals but does not confirm stress on its own. Coastal alluvial aquifers differ critically from hard rock aquifers: they have higher storativity but face the additional risk of seawater intrusion once the freshwater head declines, which can render the aquifer permanently unusable for irrigation. Always ask the user about borewell density and observed water salinity before finalising diagnosis, as saline intrusion is a distinct but related pathway in coastal settings. If SOGE is Safe but well depth is increasing and water is becoming saline, prioritise the well depth trend and salinity signal — block-level SOGE may mask localised coastal depletion near the shoreline.

confounders.confounder: Seawater intrusion causing water quality failure rather than quantity depletion as the primary constraint on irrigation
confounders.how_to_distinguish: If farmers report that water is available in wells but is too salty or leaves white deposits, the primary pathway is saline intrusion rather than volumetric groundwater depletion. Check groundwater_salinity data if available. Seawater intrusion is triggered by over-extraction in coastal aquifers but requires different solutions (managed recharge to maintain freshwater head, rather than demand reduction alone). SOGE may appear moderate while salinity renders the available water unusable.

confounders.confounder: Seasonal or inter-annual rainfall deficit (meteorological drought) causing apparent groundwater stress through reduced monsoon recharge rather than structural over-extraction
confounders.how_to_distinguish: Compare annual_precipitation_mm against the long-term baseline for the AER (900–1400mm for AER-18). If delta_g is negative only in drought years and recovers in normal rainfall years, drought is the primary driver and groundwater stress is episodic rather than chronic. Check soge_dev_percent — if it remains below 50% but delta_g is negative in drought years, drought rather than extraction is likely primary. Chronic stress is indicated when delta_g remains negative even in years with near-normal rainfall.

missing_variable_questions.question_to_user: Roughly how many borewells or tubewells are there in your village or in nearby villages? Would you say there are very few (less than 10), a moderate number (10–50), or a large number (more than 50)? Has the number increased noticeably in the last 5–10 years?
missing_variable_questions.how_answer_updates_diagnosis: High borewell density in a coastal alluvial aquifer with moderate SOGE confirms localised over-extraction not captured by the block-level SOGE figure, strongly confirming the groundwater stress pathway. Rapid recent increase in borewell numbers suggests accelerating extraction pressure that may not yet be reflected in SOGE data.

missing_variable_questions.question_to_user: Have farmers noticed that borewell or well water has become saltier, tastes different, leaves white or crusty deposits on soil or pipes, or is no longer suitable for certain crops? Has this worsened in recent years or during dry seasons?
missing_variable_questions.how_answer_updates_diagnosis: Increasing salinity in coastal alluvial aquifers indicates seawater intrusion driven by declining freshwater head — a consequence of over-extraction. This changes the diagnosis from simple volumetric depletion to saline intrusion, which requires managed aquifer recharge to restore freshwater pressure as the primary intervention, rather than demand reduction alone. Seasonal salinity (worse in dry season) is typical of coastal aquifers under extraction stress.

missing_variable_questions.question_to_user: Have farmers noticed that well or borewell depth has increased over the past several years? Can you estimate how much deeper wells are now compared to 5–8 years ago (e.g., less than 1 m, 1–3 m, more than 3 m)?
missing_variable_questions.how_answer_updates_diagnosis: A reported increase of more than 3 m over 5–8 years strongly confirms groundwater table decline and supports the groundwater stress pathway even when block-level SOGE appears Safe. Stable or shallow well depths suggest extraction pressure or drought rather than structural depletion.

sources.title: National Compilation of Dynamic Ground Water Resources of India 2022
sources.relevance_note: Defines SOGE class thresholds (Safe <70%, Semi-critical 70–90%, Critical 90–100%, Over-exploited >100%) used in sig_01; provides block-level groundwater assessment for coastal Tamil Nadu, Andhra Pradesh and Odisha

sources.title: Rapid groundwater decline and some cases of recovery in aquifers globally
sources.relevance_note: Documents rapid groundwater-level declines (>0.5 m/year) as widespread in dry regions with extensive croplands; shows 30% of regional aquifers have accelerating declines; also documents cases of recovery through managed recharge and policy change, supporting intervention recommendations

sources.title: Sixty years of global progress in managed aquifer recharge
sources.relevance_note: Documents India's 500,000+ historical tanks and ponds for recharge; describes government and NGO investment in percolation tanks in southern India as a major managed aquifer recharge strategy; supports recommendations for trench/check-dam construction and community pond repair in coastal alluvial settings

sources.title: Groundwater use for irrigation – a global inventory
sources.relevance_note: Documents India as having the largest area equipped for groundwater irrigation globally (39 million ha); establishes that groundwater use in irrigation is increasing, leading to extraction above recharge rates — directly relevant to coastal alluvial plains under intensive rabi and zaid irrigation

sources.title: Addressing Depletion in Alluvial Aquifers
sources.relevance_note: Argues that management approaches for alluvial aquifers must differ from hard rock systems; documents that abstraction far exceeds natural recharge in alluvial aquifers; supports the need for demand-side and institutional approaches (Panchayat-NGO governance) alongside supply-side SWC works
